### Pensacola Florida crash

What you're seeing here would be a fan hub section that has complete fracture and was released. There was on the news a couple weeks ago, the cover to an engine came off during flight, and the news was showing the engine vibrating back and forth. When something like this happens, a lot of pieces in the engine can be released, and oftentimes they..."

### Titanium alloy inclusion defect
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Titanium alloy inclusion defect"
- **Anchor:** `§7.p3`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "For titanium alloys there are two main types of defect. The type one defect is called a high interstitial defect, and this is an alpha region that has a higher hardness than the surrounding material. When you have a localized area of high hardness, that's where your crack initiation will occur. You want to try to avoid having brittle regions in these components that are spinning at 20,000 rpm and operating at 2,200 to 2,500 Fahrenheit and high pressures. If you have a brittle region, it often will end in a catastrophic type fracture. The brittle region and the high interstitial defects are..."

### Titanium alloy inclusion defect
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Titanium alloy inclusion defect"
- **Anchor:** `§7.p4`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "The second type is the high aluminum defect, which is an aluminum-rich alpha region that is over multiple beta grains. Depending on whether you have alpha grains or beta grains, because you have various stabilizing elements, you will have different types of defects when you look at it under a microscope based on the elements in each one of those defects. The third one is a high-density inclusion — this relates to refractory elements in the melt.

## §8. In-service damage, inspection, and degradation mechanisms [25:58]"

### Titanium alloy inclusion defect
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Titanium alloy inclusion defect"
- **Anchor:** `§12.p3`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "The hard alpha defect cracked with the application of stress during the first initial exposure to thrust. First takeoff is when the crack initiated. As I said, fluorescent penetrant was performed only 760 cycles to failure. It was still allowed to go back into service. One of the findings in the report, they noticed in the area around the inclusion there was discoloration, and the discoloration was attributed to the penetrant. That was actually a fairly damning conclusion that led to quite a few changes to the quality assurance and quality control in the plant.
